_note: One-source data file for the rich web panel (SPEC §D.10.2), side page, and ★/☆ computation. Supersedes facet_confirmation.json. Source: ~/.track-coach/explore/reference_notes.html (hand-curated 2026-06-29), curated/enriched 2026-06-30 (s29). Each direction: artist, real_name, genre_era, note, blurb, traits [{phrase, title, axis, expect, tier}], sources. Render rule: tier=direct → ★ 'measurement confirms'; tier=indirect → ☆ 'measurement confirms (indirect)'; tier=web-only → grey 'web says; our tracks don't show it'; tier=not-measurable → grey-italic 'not measurable with our axes'. _confirm_z: minimum |centroid z| for the bar-chart ★/☆ computation in render_reference_read (still dynamic).

DeepChord.genre_era: Dub techno / ambient techno — Detroit — mid-1990s to present
DeepChord.blurb: DeepChord is widely placed in the second wave of the Basic Channel sound, with a heavier lean toward ambience and field recording than most dub techno. Rod Modell starts composition with the ambient parts — processed field recordings, sustained low-frequency chord stabs, textures that can loop indefinitely. The kick drum arrives last, as a metronome underneath rather than a driving feature. The signal chain is extreme: Eventide Orville, Korg Stage Echos, spring reverbs, tape delays work on the source until, as Modell has said, "little of the original remains." The result is a wide, swirling spatial image, a deeply dark tonal palette described as "sounds like 4am," and an almost complete absence of melodic events above the held ambient layer. Tracks exist in a lava-lamp stasis — they develop through micro-variation, not an energy arc.

traits.title: Slow, meditative tempo — ~110–125 BPM, often toward the lower end; described as a "resting heartbeat of a rhythm"
Devastatingly deep sub-bass pulses as the structural anchor of the mix
Bass notes held long across bar boundaries — the dub reggae inheritance; bass is "set adrift" rather than punchy
Restrained, heavily processed dynamics — the deep FX chain compresses natural transients flat
Very wide stereo from the dub reverb and delay chain — field recordings swirling in the background
Ambient pads dominate the mix; the kick is added last as an afterthought, not the lead element
Pads sustained for very long durations — dronal, static textures that can loop forever, no melodic phrases
Dark, low-brightness tonal palette — no bright leads; processed field recordings sit below the presence range
Pad brightness in the dark / low-mid range — pad material is processed source material, not filter-open synths
Minimal drums share — kick as a metronome, not a sonic feature; drums are an afterthought
Very sparse arrangement density — near-minimalist, repetitive loops, few events per bar above the sustained layer
Pad note rate near zero — pads hold, not play; the loop rate is not a melody

Venetian Snares.genre_era: Breakcore / IDM / glitch — Winnipeg, Canada — late 1990s to present
Venetian Snares.note: Funk's style varies sharply between albums. Breakcore albums (e.g., Winnipeg Is a Frozen Shithole) and orchestral-fusion albums (e.g., Rossz Csillag Alatt Született, 2005) have almost opposite pad and brightness profiles. Traits marked as orchestral-album-specific are called out below.
Venetian Snares.blurb: Aaron Funk is widely credited with defining breakcore as a genre. The core sound is hyper-fast — 180–200+ BPM — built around mutilated amen breaks constructed in Renoise into patterns of "organized chaos." Drums dominate: the breakbeats are shredded, resequenced, and stacked into dense, manic textures that leave the mix with little headroom and constant maximum intensity. There is no conventional build arc; the energy arrives immediately and stays. Bright, staccato high-frequency elements — sci-fi synths, jittery stabs, chime accents — fill the spaces between drum hits. Odd time signatures, notably 7/4, are a recurring structural signature. In a separate body of work, Funk makes orchestral-breakcore albums sampling Bartók, Mahler, and Elgar; these have bright string layers functioning as sustained pads, producing an almost opposite tonal character to the pure breakcore albums.

traits.title: Hyper-fast tempo — 180–200+ BPM, breakcore speed
Drum texture dominates the mix — mutilated amen breaks as the primary sonic element; "meticulously complex drums"
Extreme per-bar event density — "manic breakbeats," dense sample stacking, organized chaos
Constant maximum intensity — no conventional build arc; "terrorizing barrage" from bar one
Heavily compressed / loud mastering — extreme density leaves no headroom; loudness-war zone dynamic range
Bright, staccato high-frequency content — jittery stabs, chime accents, sci-fi synths
Odd time signatures (notably 7/4) as a recurring structural device
Orchestral strings as long-sustain pad layer (on classical-fusion albums) — cello and string phrases from Bartók / Mahler samples
High synth / pad share on orchestral albums — the string layer occupies a large share of the mix
Pad brightness is high on orchestral albums — Bartók/Mahler/Elgar strings have strong presence in the 2–8 kHz range

SCSI-9.genre_era: Deep house / tech-house / minimal techno — Moscow, Russia — active from ~2001
SCSI-9.note: SCSI-9 is less covered in depth than the other two. No Wikipedia article exists; Resident Advisor reviews are the richest English-language critical source. Coverage confidence is lower, but the "hypermelodic" and "dub-effects" descriptors are consistent across multiple reviews.
SCSI-9.blurb: SCSI-9 — Moscow-based duo Anton Kubikov and Maxim Milyutenko — are among the most respected Russian electronic acts internationally, releasing on Kompakt, Force Tracks, and Traum/Trapez. Their signature is melodic foreground synth phrases sitting on top of a sparse but detailed arrangement: "wistful melodies," "bell-tone droplets," "hypermelodic tech-house." The tempo sits mid-range, around 122–128 BPM. The bass is warm and punchy — mid-bass presence rather than deep sub-bass, released between hits rather than held. Below the melody, a harmonic pad bed sits with dub effects in the background: kaleidoscopic reverb, spacey tones, echoing synths. Critics reach for the word "cheery," but in a minimal context it reads more luminous than saccharine. The character is described as "geometric but above all analog" — mathematically spare with a handmade warmth.

traits.title: Mid-tempo deep house / tech-house groove — ~122–128 BPM
Warm, present bass pulse — mid-bass presence, not sub-heavy; "bass allowed a little more time to breathe"
Bass notes released between hits — punchy, rhythmic, not dronal; breathing rather than sustained
Melodic foreground synth phrases — "wistful melodies," "hypermelodic tech-house," "bell-tone droplets," active lead lines
Ambient pad bed underneath the melody — harmonic support layer with dub effects
Wide stereo from kaleidoscopic reverb and delay — "spacey tones and a whole lot of reverb," "echoing synths"
Moderate, controlled dynamic range — not compressed flat; "lava-lamp minimalism," deliberately not frenetic
Bright pad / synth timbre — "cheery synths," "synthetic bells," "shimmering daze," upper-mid luminous quality
Mix reads bright overall — follows from the melodic synth lines and synthetic bells
Low arrangement density — "straightforward but highly detailed," geometric minimalism; events are sparse, not layered
Gentle upward energy arc over track duration — Kompakt deep-house style gradual builds (web-argued, not documented explicitly)

sources.label: Resident Advisor review — Easy as Down (2008)
Resident Advisor review — The Line of Nine (2006)
